author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans

title: Homily IV on Rom. i. 26, 27.

source.title: NPNF1-11. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Acts of the Apostles and the Epistle to the Romans

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For this cause God gave them up unto vile affections: for even their women did change the natural use into that which is against nature: and likewise also the men, leaving the natural use of the woman, burned in their lust one towards another.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: All these affections then were vile, but chiefly the mad lust after males; for the soul is more the sufferer in sins, and more dishonored, than the body in diseases.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But behold how here too, as in the case of the doctrines, he deprives them of excuse, by saying of the women, that “they changed the natural use.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For no one, he means, can say that it was by being hindered of legitimate intercourse that they came to this pass, or that it was from having no means to fulfil their desire that they were driven into this monstrous insaneness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the changing implies possession.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Which also when discoursing upon the doctrines he said, “They changed the truth of God for a lie.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And with regard to the men again, he shows the same thing by saying, “Leaving the natural use of the woman.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And in a like way with those, these he also puts out of all means of defending themselves by charging them not only that they had the means of gratification, and left that which they had, and went after another, but that having dishonored that which was natural, they ran after that which was contrary to nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But that which is contrary to nature hath in it an irksomeness and displeasingness, so that they could not fairly allege even pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For genuine pleasure is that which is according to nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But when God hath left one, then all things are turned upside down.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And thus not only was their doctrine Satanical, but their life too was diabolical.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now when he was discoursing of their doctrines, he put before them the world and man’s understanding, telling them that, by the judgment afforded them by God, they might through the things which are seen, have been led as by the hand to the Creator, and then, by not willing to do so, they remained inexcusable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Here in the place of the world he sets the pleasure according to nature, which they would have enjoyed with more sense of security and greater glad-heartedness, and so have been far removed from shameful deeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But they would not; whence they are quite out of the pale of pardon, and have done an insult to nature itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And a yet more disgraceful thing than these is it, when even the women seek after these intercourses, who ought to have more sense of shame than men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And here too the judgment of Paul is worthy of admiration, how having fallen upon two opposite matters he accomplishes them both with all exactness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he wished both to speak chastely and to sting the hearer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now both these things were not in his power to do, but one hindered the other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if you speak chastely you shall not be able to bear hard upon the hearer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if you are minded to touch him to the quick, you are forced to lay the naked facts before him in plain terms.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But his discreet and holy soul was able to do both with exactness, and by naming nature has at once given additional force to his accusation, and also used this as a sort of veil, to keep the chasteness of his description.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And next, having reproached the women first, he goes on to the men also, and says, “And likewise also the men leaving the natural use of the woman.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Which is an evident proof of the last degree of corruptness, when both sexes are abandoned, and both he that was ordained to be the instructor of the woman, and she who was bid to become an helpmate to the man, work the deeds of enemies against one another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And reflect too how significantly he uses his words.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he does not say that they were enamoured of, and lusted after one another, but, “they burned in their lust one toward another.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: You see that the whole of desire comes of an exorbitancy which endureth not to abide within its proper limits.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For everything which transgresseth the laws by God appointed, lusteth after monstrous things and not those which be customary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For as many oftentimes having left the desire of food get to feed upon earth and small stones, and others being possessed by excessive thirst often long even for mire, thus these also ran into this ebullition of lawless love.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if you say, and whence came this intensity of lust?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: It was from the desertion of God: and whence is the desertion of God? from the lawlessness of them that left Him; “men with men working that which is unseemly.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do not, he means, because you have heard that they burned, suppose that the evil was only in desire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the greater part of it came of their luxuriousness, which also kindled into flame their lust.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is why he did not say being swept along or being overtaken, an expression he uses elsewhere; but what? working.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: They made a business of the sin, and not only a business, but even one zealously followed up.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And he called it not lust, but that which is unseemly, and that properly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For they both dishonored nature, and trampled on the laws.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And see the great confusion which fell out on both sides.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For not only was the head turned downwards but the feet too were upwards, and they became enemies to themselves and to one another, bringing in a pernicious kind of strife, and one even more lawless than any civil war, and one rife in divisions, and of varied form.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For they divided this into four new, and lawless kinds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Since (3 mss. whence) this war was not twofold or threefold, but even fourfold.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Consider then.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: It was meet, that the twain should be one, I mean the woman and the man.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For “the twain,” it says, “shall be one flesh.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But this the desire of intercourse effected, and united the sexes to one another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This desire the devil having taken away, and having turned the course thereof into another fashion, he thus sundered the sexes from one another, and made the one to become two parts in opposition to the law of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it says, “the two shall be one flesh;” but he divided the one flesh into two: here then is one war.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Again, these same two parts he provoked to war both against themselves and against one another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For even women again abused women, and not men only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And the men stood against one another, and against the female sex, as happens in a battle by night.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: You see a second and third war, and a fourth and fifth; there is also another, for beside what have been mentioned they also behaved lawlessly against nature itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For when the Devil saw that this desire it is, principally, which draws the sexes together, he was bent on cutting through the tie, so as to destroy the race, not only by their not copulating lawfully, but also by their being stirred up to war, and in sedition against one another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “And receiving in themselves that recompense of their error which was meet.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: See how he goes again to the fountain head of the evil, namely, the impiety that comes of their doctrines, and this he says is a reward of that lawlessness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For since in speaking of hell and punishment, it seemed he would not at present be credible to the ungodly and deliberate choosers of such a life, but even scorned, he shows that the punishment was in this pleasure itself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: (So Plato Theæt. p.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if they perceive it not, but are still pleased, be not amazed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For even they that are mad, and are afflicted with phrenzy (cf.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 265–277) while doing themselves much injury and making themselves such objects of compassion, that others weep over them themselves smile and revel over what has happened.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet we do not only for this not say that they are quit of punishment, but for this very reason are under a more grievous vengeance, in that they are unconscious of the plight they are in.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it is not the disordered but those who are sound whose votes one has to gain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet of old the matter seemed even to be a law, and a certain law-giver among them bade the domestic slaves neither to use unguents when dry (i.e. except in bathing) nor to keep youths, giving the free this place of honor, or rather of shamefulness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet they, however, did not think the thing shameful, but as being a grand privilege, and one too great for slaves, the Athenian people, the wisest of people, and Solon who is so great amongst them, permitted it to the free alone.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And sundry other books of the philosophers may one see full of this disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But we do not therefore say that the thing was made lawful, but that they who received this law were pitiable, and objects for many tears.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For these are treated in the same way as women that play the whore.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Or rather their plight is more miserable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For in the case of the one the intercourse, even if lawless, is yet according to nature: but this is contrary both to law and nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For even if there were no hell, and no punishment had been threatened, this were worse than any punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet if you say “they found pleasure in it,” you tell me what adds to the vengeance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For suppose I were to see a person running naked, with his body all besmeared with mire, and yet not covering himself, but exulting in it, I should not rejoice with him, but should rather bewail that he did not even perceive that he was doing shamefully.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But that I may show the atrocity in a yet clearer light, bear with me in one more example.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now if any one condemned a virgin to live in close dens (θαλομευομένην), and to have intercourse with unreasoning brutes, and then she was pleased with such intercourse, would she not for this be especially a worthy object of tears, as being unable to be freed from this misery owing to her not even perceiving the misery?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: It is plain surely to every one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if that were a grievous thing, neither is this less so than that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For to be insulted by one’s own kinsmen is more piteous than to be so by strangers: these I say (5 mss.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “I consider”) are even worse than murderers: since to die even is better than to live under such insolency.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the murderer dissevers the soul from the body, but this man ruins the soul with the body.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And name what sin you will, none will you mention equal to this lawlessness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And if they that suffer such things perceived them, they would accept ten thousand deaths so they might not suffer this evil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For there is not, there surely is not, a more grievous evil than this insolent dealing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if when discoursing about fornication Paul said, that “Every sin which a man doeth is without the body, but he that committeth fornication sinneth against his own body” (1 Cor. vi.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 18); what shall we say of this madness, which is so much worse than fornication as cannot even be expressed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For I should not only say that thou hast become a woman, but that thou hast lost thy manhood, and hast neither changed into that nature nor kept that which thou haddest, but thou hast been a traitor to both of them at once, and deserving both of men and women to be driven out and stoned, as having wronged either sex.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And that thou mayest learn what the real force of this is, if any one were to come and assure you that he would make you a dog instead of being a man, would you not flee from him as a plague?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But, lo! thou hast not made thyself a dog out of a man, but an animal more disgraceful than this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this is useful unto service, but he that hath thus given himself up is serviceable for nothing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Or again, if any one threatened to make men travail and be brought to bed, should we not be filled with indignation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But lo! now they that have run into this fury have done more grievously by themselves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it is not the same thing to change into the nature of women, as to continue a man and yet to have become a woman; or rather neither this nor that.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if you would know the enormity of the evil from other grounds, ask on what account the lawgivers punish them that make men eunuchs, and you will see that it is absolutely for no other reason than because they mutilate nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And yet the injustice they do is nothing to this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For there have been those that were mutilated and were in many cases useful after their mutilation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But nothing can there be more worthless than a man who has pandered himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For not the soul only, but the body also of one who hath been so treated, is disgraced, and deserves to be driven out everywhere.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: How many hells shall be enough for such?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if thou scoffest at hearing of hell and believest not that fire, remember Sodom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For we have seen, surely we have seen, even in this present life, a semblance of hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For since many would utterly disbelieve the things to come after the resurrection, hearing now of an unquenchable fire, God brings them to a right mind by things present.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For such is the burning of Sodom, and that conflagration!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And they know it well that have been at the place, and have seen with their eyes that scourge divinely sent, and the effect of the lightnings from above.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Consider how great is that sin, to have forced hell to appear even before its time!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For whereas many thought scorn of His words, by His deeds did God show them the image thereof in a certain novel way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For that rain was unwonted, for that the intercourse was contrary to nature, and it deluged the land, since lust had done so with their souls.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Wherefore also the rain was the opposite of the customary rain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now not only did it fail to stir up the womb of the earth to the production of fruits, but made it even useless for the reception of seed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For such was also the intercourse of the men, making a body of this sort more worthless than the very land of Sodom.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And what is there more detestable than a man who hath pandered himself, or what more execrable?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Oh, what madness!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Oh, what distraction!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Whence came this lust lewdly revelling and making man’s nature all that enemies could? or even worse than that, by as much as the soul is better than the body.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Oh, ye that were more senseless than irrational creatures, and more shameless than dogs! for in no case does such intercourse take place with them, but nature acknowledgeth her own limits.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But ye have even made our race dishonored below things irrational, by such indignities inflicted upon and by each other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Whence then were these evils born?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Of luxury; of not knowing God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For so soon as any have cast out the fear of Him, all that is good straightway goes to ruin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now, that this may not happen, let us keep clear before our eyes the fear of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For nothing, surely nothing, so ruins a man as to slip from this anchor, as nothing saves so much as continually looking thereto.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if by having a man before our eyes we feel more backward at doing sins, and often even through feeling abashed at servants of a better stamp we keep from doing anything amiss, consider what safety we shall enjoy by having God before our eyes!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For in no case will the Devil attack us when so conditioned, in that he would be laboring without profit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But should he see us wandering abroad, and going about without a bridle, by getting a beginning in ourselves he will be able to drive us off afterwards any whither.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And as it happens with thoughtless servants at market, who leave the needful services which their masters have entrusted to them, and rivet themselves at a mere haphazard to those who fall in their way, and waste out their leisure there; this also we undergo when we depart from the commandments of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For we presently get standing on, admiring riches, and beauty of person, and the other things which we have no business with, just as those servants attend to the beggars that do jugglers’ feats, and then, arriving too late, have to be grievously beaten at home.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And many pass the road set before them through following others, who are behaving in the same unseemly way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But let not us so do.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For we have been sent to dispatch many affairs that are urgent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And if we leave those, and stand gaping at these useless things, all our time will be wasted in vain and to no profit, and we shall suffer the extreme of punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if you wish yourself to be busy, you have whereat you ought to wonder, and to gape all your days, things which are no subject for laughter, but for wondering and manifold praises.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: As he that admires things ridiculous, will himself often be such, and even worse than he that occasioneth the laughter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And that you may not fall into this, spring away from it forthwith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For why is it, pray, that you stand gaping and fluttering at sight of riches?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What do you see so wonderful, and able to fix your eyes upon them? these gold-harnessed horses, these lackeys, partly savages, and partly eunuchs, and costly raiment, and the soul that is getting utterly soft in all this, and the haughty brow, and the bustlings, and the noise?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And wherein do these things deserve wonder? what are they better than the beggars that dance and pipe in the market-place?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For these too being taken with a sore famine of virtue, dance a dance more ridiculous than theirs, led and carried round at one time to costly tables, at another to the lodging of prostitute women, and at another to a swarm of flatterers and a host of hangers-on.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if they do wear gold, this is why they are the most pitiable, because the things which are nothing to them, are most the subject of their eager desire.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do not now, I pray, look at their raiment, but open their soul, and consider if it is not full of countless wounds, and clad with rags, and destitute, and defenceless!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What then is the use of this madness of shows? for it were much better to be poor and living in virtue, than to be a king with wickedness; since the poor man in himself enjoys all the delights of the soul, and doth not even perceive his outward poverty for his inward riches.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But the king, luxurious in those things which do not at all belong to him, is punished in those things which are his most real concern, even the soul, the thoughts, and the conscience, which are to go away with him to the other world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Since then we know these things, let us lay aside the gilded raiment, let us take up virtue and the pleasure which comes thereof.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For so, both here and hereafter, shall we come to enjoy great delights, through the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, through Whom, and with Whom, be glory to the Father, with the Holy Spirit, for ever and ever.
